section.title: The Prosperity of the Wicked
section.sectionRationale: Opening Book III with Asaph's wisdom crisis over the easy, carefree prosperity of the wicked, this psalm's turning point, 'until I entered God's sanctuary, then I discerned their end,' models how nearness to God, not mere reasoning, resolves the believer's hardest doubts.
section.thesis: Watching the wicked prosper nearly toppled Asaph's faith, until entering God's presence reordered his perspective, leaving him with a confession that outlasts every earthly loss: God is the strength of his heart and his portion forever.
section.frameNote: Five movements: an opening confession and near stumble, verses 1 to 3; the prosperity of the wicked described, verses 4 to 12; the crisis and turning point in the sanctuary, verses 13 to 17; the wicked's hidden fate unveiled, verses 18 to 22; renewed nearness to God, verses 23 to 28.

verses.text: A Psalm of Asaph. Surely God is good to Israel, to those who are pure in heart.
But as for me, my feet had almost stumbled; my steps had nearly slipped.
For I envied the arrogant when I saw the prosperity of the wicked.
They have no struggle in their death; their bodies are well-fed.
They are free of the burdens others carry; they are not afflicted like other men.
Therefore pride is their necklace; a garment of violence covers them.
From their prosperity proceeds iniquity; the imaginations of their hearts run wild.
They mock and speak with malice; with arrogance they threaten oppression.
They set their mouths against the heavens, and their tongues strut across the earth.
So their people return to this place and drink up waters in abundance.
The wicked say, "How can God know? Does the Most High have knowledge?"
Behold, these are the wicked, always carefree as they increase their wealth.
Surely in vain I have kept my heart pure; in innocence I have washed my hands.
For I am afflicted all day long and punished every morning.
If I had said, "I will speak this way," then I would have betrayed Your children.
When I tried to understand all this, it was troublesome in my sight
until I entered God's {{miqdash|sanctuary}}; then I discerned their end.
Surely You set them on {{chalaqot|slick}} ground; You cast them down into ruin.
How suddenly they are laid waste, completely swept away by terrors!
Like one waking from a dream, so You, O Lord, awaken and despise their form.
When my heart was grieved and I was pierced within,
I was senseless and ignorant; I was a brute beast before You.
Yet I am always with You; You hold my right hand.
You guide me with Your counsel, and later receive me in glory.
Whom have I in heaven but You? And on earth I desire no one besides You.
My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever.
Those far from You will surely perish; You destroy all who are unfaithful to You.
But as for me, it is good to draw near to God. I have made the Lord GOD my refuge, that I may proclaim all Your works.

miqdash.gloss: Sanctuary, holy place, the place set apart for God's presence. Asaph's crisis of faith is not resolved by more reasoning but by entering this place, where God's presence reorders how he sees the wicked's apparent success.

chalaqot.gloss: Slippery, slick places. What looked like solid ground under the wicked's feet is revealed, once seen from the sanctuary, to be treacherously unstable, a picture of security that can collapse in an instant.

basic.context: Opening Book III, this psalm of Asaph honestly confesses a near collapse of faith brought on by watching arrogant, wicked people live carefree, wealthy, and untroubled lives, until entering God's sanctuary reorders his whole perspective.
basic.hermeneutics: This is an honest wisdom psalm wrestling with the classic problem of the prosperity of the wicked; it does not deny the reality of that prosperity but resolves the crisis not through argument but through nearness to God.
basic.originalLanguages: The turning point comes when Asaph enters God's miqdash, sanctuary, verse 17, and discovers the wicked stand on chalaqot, slippery ground, verse 18, ground that looked solid but was never secure at all.
basic.typology: Asaph's confession, 'Whom have I in heaven but You,' anticipates the greater satisfaction found only in Christ, and his hope of later being 'received in glory' anticipates the believer's union with the risen Christ who has gone before to prepare a place.
basic.christ: Asaph's cry, 'God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever,' is answered fully in Christ, in whom believers are promised an inheritance that cannot perish, spoil, or fade, 1 Peter 1:4, and whose own resurrection guarantees that being 'received in glory' is no mere hope but a certainty.
basic.god: God is shown as the only true and lasting good, One who holds the psalmist's hand through confusion, guides him with counsel, and receives him in glory, even while the seemingly secure prosperity of the wicked proves to be built on nothing.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Peter 1:4 promises an inheritance that never perishes, echoing Asaph's 'portion forever.' Philippians 3:8 counts everything as loss compared to knowing Christ, matching Asaph's 'whom have I in heaven but You.' Hebrews 12:2 shows looking to Jesus as the resolution for weary, doubting faith.
basic.oneStory: This wisdom crisis over the prosperity of the wicked, resolved only in the sanctuary of God's presence, threads into the whole Bible's story of a people learning that true and lasting good is found in God alone, a truth secured forever in Christ, our portion who never fails.

context.literary: A wisdom psalm structured around the word 'surely,' verses 1, 13, 18, contrasting what seemed sure to Asaph with what is actually sure in God's plan, moving from confession to crisis to a sanctuary-shaped resolution.
context.historical: Attributed to Asaph, a Levitical choir leader appointed by David, this psalm likely reflects an experience of Israel's monarchy period, when trade wealth created visible, sometimes ruthless prosperity among the powerful while the faithful often suffered.
context.canonical: Standing at the head of Book III, this psalm sets the tone for a collection deeply concerned with theodicy and national suffering, and its resolution in the sanctuary anticipates the fuller access to God's presence secured in Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To honestly confess a near-collapse of faith caused by envying the wicked's prosperity, and to testify that entering God's presence, not clever argument, is what restores right perspective and unshakeable hope.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes one specific crisis of doubt and its resolution; it prescribes, as an abiding pattern, bringing honest doubts into God's presence rather than either suppressing them or abandoning faith because of them.

debates.question: What exactly did Asaph 'discern' about the wicked's end upon entering the sanctuary, verse 17?

views.label: A revelation of their imminent, this-worldly downfall
views.case: Verses 18-20 describe sudden ruin, being swept away by terrors, language that fits historical reversals of fortune within this life.

views.label: A revelation of final, eschatological judgment beyond this life
views.case: The language of being 'destroyed' and contrasted with Asaph's own hope of being 'received in glory,' verse 24, suggests a final reckoning that goes beyond any single earthly setback.

debates.bearing: Both readings cohere: the sanctuary reveals that the wicked's security, whether it collapses now or finally at judgment, was never real, while the righteous, however afflicted now, are secure forever in God.

study.originalLanguages: Asaph's crisis turns only once he enters God's miqdash, sanctuary, verse 17, where he discerns that the wicked stand on chalaqot, slippery ground, verse 18, ground that appeared solid but was never actually secure.

typology.type: "Whom have I in heaven but You," verse 25
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, for whom Paul counted all things as loss, Philippians 3:8.
Anticipates the believer's certain future glory secured through union with the risen Christ, who has gone to prepare a place, John 14:2-3.

study.christ: Asaph's confession, 'God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever,' verse 26, finds its full guarantee in Christ, in whom believers receive 'an inheritance that can never perish, spoil, or fade,' 1 Peter 1:4. His hope of being 'received in glory,' verse 24, is secured by Christ's own resurrection and ascension, which opened the way for His people to follow Him into glory.
study.god: God is the psalmist's only true and lasting good, holding his hand through doubt, guiding him with counsel, and receiving him in glory, in sharp contrast to the wicked's prosperity, which proves to stand on ground that was never secure.

crossReferences.note: Paul counts everything as loss compared to knowing Christ, echoing Asaph's 'whom have I in heaven but You.'
Promises believers an inheritance that never perishes, fulfilling Asaph's confession that God is his portion forever.
Points weary, doubting believers to fix their eyes on Jesus, the true resolution for the crisis of faith Asaph describes.

study.oneStory: Asaph's near collapse of faith over the wicked's prosperity, resolved only in the nearness of God's sanctuary, threads into the whole Bible's story of learning that true and lasting good is found in God alone, a portion secured forever in Christ, who never fails or perishes.

sermon.title: Battling the Unbelief of Despondency
sermon.why: Preached from Psalm 73:21-26, Piper works through Asaph's failing flesh and heart to show that unbelief, not circumstance alone, is the root of yielding to despondency, and that God remains the strength of the heart even when it fails.

quiz.q: What nearly caused Asaph's feet to stumble, verses 2-3?

options.text: Envying the arrogant when he saw the prosperity of the wicked.
options.note: This is the psalm's opening confession and the crisis it wrestles with throughout.

options.text: A physical illness that weakened his body.
options.note: His crisis was one of faith and perspective, not primarily physical illness.

options.text: Being exiled from Jerusalem.
options.note: The text does not mention exile; the crisis is envy over the wicked's ease.

options.text: Losing an argument with a false prophet.
options.note: No such argument is described; the struggle is internal envy and doubt.

quiz.q: What finally changed Asaph's perspective on the wicked's prosperity, verse 17?

options.text: Entering God's sanctuary, where he discerned their end.
options.note: This is the psalm's decisive turning point, resolving the crisis through nearness to God rather than argument alone.

options.text: Reading a letter from a friend.
options.note: No such letter appears; the turning point is entering the sanctuary.

options.text: Winning a great military victory.
options.note: The psalm describes no battle; the resolution is spiritual and comes through God's presence.

options.text: Simply forgetting about the wicked entirely.
options.note: He does not merely forget them; he gains a clear-eyed view of their true, fragile end.

quiz.q: What does Asaph discover about the ground the wicked stand on, verse 18?

options.text: It is slick, slippery ground on which God will cast them down into ruin.
options.note: Their apparent security proves treacherous and unstable once seen from the sanctuary.

options.text: It is the most secure and fertile land in Israel.
options.note: The opposite; their standing is revealed as precarious, not secure.

options.text: It belongs rightfully to them forever.
options.note: The psalm reveals their standing to be temporary and unstable, not a lasting possession.

options.text: It is protected by their own wealth.
options.note: Their wealth does not protect them; God Himself sets them on unstable ground.

quiz.q: What confession does Asaph make in verse 26 about his own weakness?

options.text: "My flesh and my heart may fail, but God is the strength of my heart and my portion forever."
options.note: This is the psalm's central confession of confidence beyond physical and emotional collapse.

options.text: "My flesh and heart are stronger than any trouble."
options.note: He openly admits his flesh and heart may fail; his hope rests in God, not his own strength.

options.text: "I no longer need God now that I am wise."
options.note: The opposite; his confidence deepens in dependence on God, not independence from Him.

options.text: "I will seek my portion in wealth like the wicked."
options.note: He explicitly rejects this path, having learned that God alone, not wealth, is his true portion.

quiz.q: How does this psalm's resolution connect to the New Testament?

options.text: Paul similarly counts all things as loss compared to knowing Christ, echoing 'whom have I in heaven but You,' Philippians 3:8.
options.note: Asaph's confession anticipates the surpassing worth believers find in Christ alone.

options.text: It predicts the exact wealth Solomon would accumulate.
options.note: The psalm makes no such prediction; its focus is Asaph's own crisis of faith.

options.text: It has no connection to New Testament teaching.
options.note: Its language and resolution are directly echoed in passages like Philippians 3 and 1 Peter 1.

options.text: It teaches that wealth is always sinful.
options.note: The psalm critiques wicked arrogance fueled by wealth, not wealth itself as inherently sinful.

questions: Asaph nearly stumbled from envying the carefree prosperity of the arrogant, verses 2-3. Whose apparent ease or success tempts you most to doubt God's goodness?

His crisis was resolved not by winning an argument but by entering God's sanctuary, verse 17. What would it look like this week to bring your hardest doubts into God's presence rather than trying to reason your way out alone?
Asaph discovers the wicked stand on ground that only looks secure, verse 18. Where in your own life have you mistaken something fragile for something solid?
He confesses that God is the strength of his heart even when flesh and heart fail, verse 26. What losses or weaknesses in your life most need this confession right now?

Asaph concludes it is good to draw near to God and make Him his refuge, verse 28. What single step could you take today to draw nearer to God rather than nearer to the things that only seem to offer security?

translationNotes.note: The phrase rendered 'from their prosperity proceeds iniquity' is literally 'their eye bulges with fatness' in Hebrew, a vivid picture of gross, self-satisfied excess; some ancient versions read instead 'from their callous heart proceeds iniquity.'
'Their people' can also be rendered 'his people,' a small textual ambiguity that does not change the picture of a crowd drawn to and swayed by the wicked's apparent success.
'The strength of my heart' translates a Hebrew word literally meaning 'rock,' the same image used elsewhere in the Psalms for God as an unmoving foundation when every other support gives way.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via biblehub.com/bsb/psalms/73.htm (the makor-scripture toolkit's SQLite database could not be read reliably in this session's sandboxed environment; biblehub's BSB page was used as the documented fallback source). Em dash in verse 12 removed and replaced with a comma; no em or en dashes remain. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming John Piper's 'Battling the Unbelief of Despondency' at desiringgod.org, preached from Psalm 73:21-26.
